01_initial_request.input_data: Measured reaction fluxes, metabolic network stoichiometry, and physicochemical constraints
01_initial_request.user_intent: To develop methods for resolving infeasibility in Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) scenarios caused by inconsistent measured fluxes
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: A feasible flux distribution obtained through minimal corrections to the given flux values

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] FBA scenarios with measured fluxes may become infeasible due to inconsistencies between measurements and network constraints. [Gap] Need to detect whether the current FBA system with fixed fluxes is feasible or infeasible. [Decision] To achieve infeasibility detection, chose to use LP solver to perform FBA optimization on the metabolic network with fixed flux constraints, expecting to obtain feasibility status from solver output.

parameters.constraints: Base constraints (1)-(3) plus fixed flux constraints (5)
parameters.objective: FBA objective function from (4) or removed for feasibility check

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Infeasibility message from LP solver when trying to solve the system with fixed fluxes

references.citation: Orth et al., 2010, What is flux balance analysis?
references.relevance: Provides foundation for FBA methodology and constraint-based modeling

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Infeasibility has been detected in the FBA scenario. [Gap] Need to identify which reactions have fixed/known flux values that may be causing inconsistencies. [Decision] To achieve identification of fixed reactions, chose to use model analysis tool to search for reactions where lower and upper bounds are identical, expecting to obtain set F of reactions with fixed rates.

parameters.search_criteria: Reactions where lower bound equals upper bound

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Set F of reactions with fixed (known) flux values identified, with k = |F| known reaction rates

references.citation: Klamt et al., 2008, Structural and functional analysis of cellular networks with CellNetAnalyzer
references.relevance: Describes CellNetAnalyzer toolbox for metabolic network analysis

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Fixed reactions have been identified. [Gap] Need to determine if algebraic redundancies exist in the steady-state constraints that contribute to infeasibility. [Decision] To achieve redundancy analysis, chose to use classical MFA calculation based on pseudoinverse to compute degrees of redundancy using Equation (15), expecting to obtain degR value and identify redundant rates via redundancy matrix.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Degrees of redundancy (degR) calculated and redundant reaction rates identified from non-zero columns in redundancy matrix R

references.citation: Stephanopoulos et al., 1993, Metabolic Engineering: Principles and Methodology
references.relevance: Provides classical MFA methodology and least-squares approaches

references.citation: Strang, 1980, Linear Algebra and Its Applications

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Redundancies and fixed reactions have been characterized. [Gap] Need to select appropriate optimization approach and weighting scheme for computing minimal corrections. [Decision] To achieve method selection, chose to use decision framework based on available solvers and measurement variance knowledge, expecting to obtain selected optimization method (QP or LP) and weighting scheme (W1, W2, or W3).

parameters.recommendation: QP with W1 if variances known and QP solver available; otherwise LP with W2

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Optimization approach (QP or LP) and weighting scheme selected based on available information and solver capabilities

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Optimization method and weights have been selected. [Gap] Need to compute minimal corrections to measured fluxes to make the system feasible. [Decision] To achieve correction computation via QP, chose to use QP solver to minimize weighted sum of squared corrections using Equation (21), expecting to obtain correction values δ_i for each measured flux.

parameters.weights: w_i based on selected weighting scheme (W1, W2, or W3)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Correction values δ_i computed for known reaction rates, yielding unique solution at minimum of objective function

references.relevance: Provides theoretical foundation for quadratic programming

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] QP approach has been defined as alternative method. [Gap] Need to compute minimal corrections using LP approach as alternative to QP. [Decision] To achieve correction computation via LP, chose to use LP solver to minimize weighted sum of absolute corrections using Equation (24) with slack variables, expecting to obtain correction values for measured fluxes.

parameters.slack_variables: δ_i+ ≥ 0, δ_i- ≥ 0 for each measured rate
parameters.weights: w_i based on selected weighting scheme

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Correction values computed via LP approach; may have non-unique solutions with equal weights but more likely unique with W1 or W2

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Corrections have been computed using QP or LP approach. [Gap] Need to apply corrections and verify the system is now feasible with uniquely determined rates identified. [Decision] To achieve feasibility verification and rate determination, chose to use FVA on the corrected system to identify uniquely determined rates and feasible flux ranges, expecting to obtain feasible flux distribution with corrected bounds.

parameters.corrected_bounds: Applied corrections δ_i to measured fluxes
parameters.precision_adjustment: Slightly lower precision if numerical problems occur
parameters.uniquely_determined: Reactions with identical lower and upper bounds from FVA

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Feasible flux distribution obtained; uniquely determined rates identified via FVA (identical lower and upper bounds)

references.citation: Mahadevan & Schilling, 2003, The effects of alternate optimal solutions in constraint-based genome-scale metabolic models
references.relevance: Describes FVA methodology for determining flux ranges

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Methods have been developed and implemented. [Gap] Need to validate the approaches on realistic metabolic models to assess performance. [Decision] To achieve validation, chose to use CellNetAnalyzer/CNApy with CPLEX and MATLAB solvers on E. coli core (ECC2comp) and genome-scale (iJO1366) models with three problem types (P1-P3), expecting to obtain computation time and success rate metrics.

parameters.problems: P1 (coupled reactions), P2 (growth + carbon exchange), P3 (inconsistent FBA solution)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: LP-based approach completed within 1 second for all problems; QP with CPLEX completed within 1 second; QP with MATLAB succeeded for core model but failed for 5/6 genome-scale problems

references.citation: Hädicke & Klamt, 2017, EColiCore2: A reference model of the central metabolism of Escherichia coli
references.relevance: Provides ECC2comp core model used for testing

references.citation: Orth et al., 2011, A comprehensive genome-scale reconstruction of Escherichia coli metabolism
references.relevance: Provides iJO1366 genome-scale model used for testing

references.citation: Thiele et al., 2022, CNApy: A CellNetAnalyzer GUI in Python
references.relevance: Describes CNApy implementation of the methods

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Testing on E. coli core (ECC2comp) and genome-scale (iJO1366) metabolic models with three infeasibility problem types (P1-P3)

LP_computation_time.interpretation: LP-based approach successfully completed within one second for all tested problems in both core and genome-scale models

QP_CPLEX_computation_time.interpretation: QP-based approach with CPLEX solver completed within one second for all problems in both networks

QP_MATLAB_success_rate_genome_scale.interpretation: MATLAB's QP solver succeeded for only one out of six tested genome-scale problems, indicating LP may be more practical for large-scale models

feasibility_achievement.interpretation: All methods successfully produced feasible flux distributions through minimal corrections to measured flux values

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. Both QP-based (minimizing weighted squared corrections) and LP-based (minimizing weighted absolute corrections) methods successfully resolve infeasibility in FBA scenarios caused by inconsistent measured fluxes. The methods provide feasible flux distributions through minimal corrections, with LP approach showing better practical performance for genome-scale models when using standard solvers.
